metadata.title: The Alignment Problem: Machine Learning and Human Values — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Fairness constraints can collide as a mathematical impossibility theorem, not a fixable software bug. The reward hypothesis claims all goals reduce to maximizing a single scalar value. That elegance is almost suspicious, since human motivation seems unlikely to be so simple. On competing values the book delivers clarity about trade-offs rather than false consensus.

artifactLines: Fairness constraints can collide as a mathematical impossibility theorem, not a fixable software bug.
The reward hypothesis claims all goals reduce to maximizing a single scalar value.
That elegance is almost suspicious, since human motivation seems unlikely to be so simple.
On competing values the book delivers clarity about trade-offs rather than false consensus.

beats.narration_text: The idea that fairness limits aren't a software bug but a mathematical impossibility theorem, tied to the suspiciously elegant reward hypothesis that all goals reduce to maximizing a scalar, is what I want to interrogate. Help me: (1) explain which fairness metrics provably cannot be satisfied at once and why; (2) unpack what the reward hypothesis assumes and where it feels too simple; (3) show how 'clarity about trade-offs' rather than consensus is the honest outcome for alignment.
